# Architecture



Runtime [#runtime]

* TypeScript / Node.js CLI
* zvec canonical store under `.ragit/store/`
* Memory control plane under `.ragit/memory/`
* Searchable long-term memory corpus under `docs/memory/`
* Local placeholder embedding provider for v1
* Git-tracked manifests under `.ragit/manifest/`

Retrieval [#retrieval]

* Chunk embeddings + keyword scoring
* Weighted hybrid ranking (`alpha`)
* Point-in-time query with snapshot SHA

Binding model [#binding-model]

* Every snapshot binds to commit SHA
* Incremental ingest supports `--since <sha>`
* Hooks (`post-commit`, `post-merge`) keep index fresh
* `memory wrap` updates working state without changing snapshot contracts
* `memory promote` writes tracked docs into `docs/memory/**` and can ingest them against the current `HEAD`

If you are reading this as a user rather than an implementer, the conceptual explanation lives in <a href="/en/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a>.


# Overview



RAGit is a local-first RAG CLI for git-based AI agent workflows.
It captures project documents and context, then binds retrieval snapshots to commit SHA.

Product Purpose [#product-purpose]

RAGit is a local-first RAG CLI that turns AI agent project documents and context into commit-bound, reusable knowledge inside the repository.

Core Value [#core-value]

* Preserve project context across AI agent work
* Reproduce knowledge at a specific commit state
* Turn structured docs into agent-ready inputs
* Automate indexing without adding workflow friction

License [#license]

RAGit is licensed under Apache-2.0.
The root [LICENSE](https://github.com/rhiokim/ragit/blob/main/LICENSE) file is the single source of truth for license terms across this repository.

What You Get [#what-you-get]

* Commit-bound retrieval snapshots
* Hybrid search (vector + keyword)
* Agent-friendly context packing
* A split memory model for active work and durable knowledge
* Secure ingestion with secret masking

Supported Document Types [#supported-document-types]

Document types are not storage formats. They tell RAGit what role a document plays inside collaborative memory, so the next agent can tell whether a file is about product intent, system requirements, implementation contracts, decisions, or topology.
The acronyms below are shorthand for the canonical document names, so first-time readers can map the role and the label together immediately.

| Type                                  | Use it when                                                                      | Main output                                | Common boundary                                                              |
| ------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Architecture Decision (ADR)`         | You need to record a decision and why you chose it                               | Decision, rationale, consequences          | Not an execution checklist like `Plan`                                       |
| `Product Requirement (PRD)`           | You are defining the product problem, users, goals, and success criteria         | Product intent and acceptance outcomes     | Not a system contract like `Software Requirements (SRS)`                     |
| `Software Requirements (SRS)`         | You are defining what the system must do end to end                              | Functional and non-functional requirements | Not implementation detail like `Implementation Specification (SPEC)`         |
| `Implementation Specification (SPEC)` | You are pinning down module behavior, interfaces, state, and validation          | Implementation contract                    | Not topology explanation like `Phase and Binding Documents (PBD)`            |
| `Plan`                                | You are organizing milestones, tasks, and delivery sequence                      | Execution plan and work breakdown          | Not a durable decision record like `ADR`                                     |
| `Domain-Driven Design (DDD)`          | You are modeling bounded contexts, aggregates, and domain structure              | Domain model and conceptual boundaries     | Not a simple term dictionary like `Glossary`                                 |
| `Glossary`                            | You need one stable meaning for recurring terms                                  | Shared vocabulary                          | Not a domain model or architecture map like `DDD`                            |
| `Phase and Binding Documents (PBD)`   | You are explaining phase topology, bindings, interaction paths, and drift points | Topology and binding map                   | Not a feature-by-feature contract like `Implementation Specification (SPEC)` |

Quick Distinctions [#quick-distinctions]

* `Product Requirement (PRD)` vs `Software Requirements (SRS)`: `PRD` answers why the product exists and what outcome matters. `SRS` answers what the system must provide.
* `Software Requirements (SRS)` vs `Implementation Specification (SPEC)`: `SRS` stays at the system contract level. `SPEC` moves down to implementation behavior, interfaces, and acceptance details.
* `Implementation Specification (SPEC)` vs `Phase and Binding Documents (PBD)`: `SPEC` explains what one implementation unit must do. `PBD` explains how phases and bindings are arranged across units.
* `Architecture Decision (ADR)` vs `Plan`: `ADR` records a stable decision. `Plan` records the sequence of work to execute.
* `Domain-Driven Design (DDD)` vs `Glossary`: `DDD` models structure and boundaries in the domain. `Glossary` stabilizes the meaning of recurring terms.

Why RAGit Keeps SPEC and PBD Separate [#why-ragit-keeps-spec-and-pbd-separate]

* `SPEC` is optimized for questions like "what should this module do?" and "what interface contract must be preserved?"
* `PBD` is optimized for questions like "how do phases connect?" and "where can drift or failure happen between parts?"
* Keeping them separate makes retrieval more precise for agents that need either feature-level contracts or topology-level reasoning.

Where To Start [#where-to-start]

* If you are defining a new feature or workflow, start with `PRD`, then move to `SRS`, and finally `SPEC`.
* If you already know the feature and need to explain one implementation unit, start with `SPEC`.
* If the hard part is coordination between phases, components, or agents, add a `PBD`.
* If you are locking in a choice between alternatives, write an `ADR`.
* If the team keeps reusing the same term with different meanings, add a `Glossary`. If the confusion is about domain boundaries, add `DDD`.

Next [#next]

Read <a href="/en/docs/quickstart">Quickstart</a> to run the docs and CLI workflows.
If you are setting up the authoring system inside a repository, read <a href="/en/docs/init-guide">Init Guide</a>.
If you want command-by-command guidance with examples and option details, start at <a href="/en/docs/commands">Commands</a>.
If you want to understand why memory is split across control-plane state and searchable docs, read <a href="/en/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a>.
If you are integrating RAGit into an agent workflow, read <a href="/en/docs/agent-cli">Agent CLI Contract</a> for `--format json`, `--view minimal`, `--input`, and `--dry-run` rules.


# Quickstart



Requirements [#requirements]

* Node.js 20+
* pnpm 10+
* Git repository

Install [#install]

For repository-local development:

```bash
pnpm install
```

Inside a repository checkout, run CLI commands with the project script entry for your selected package manager.
To confirm that the local CLI entry is wired correctly, run:

```bash
pnpm ragit --help
```

The local build step is optional and only needed when you want to generate the `dist/` bundle locally.

```bash
pnpm build
```

For the published CLI:

```bash
npm install -g ragit
pnpm add -g ragit
bun add -g ragit
npx ragit --help
```

Initialize RAGit [#initialize-ragit]

```bash
pnpm ragit init
pnpm ragit hooks install
pnpm ragit ingest --all
```

At this point you can start using commit-bound retrieval. If you want the philosophy behind `memory wrap`, `memory recall`, and `memory promote`, read <a href="/en/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a> before you add memory workflows to your project.
If you want detailed command syntax and per-command examples, continue with <a href="/en/docs/commands">Commands</a>.

Run docs locally [#run-docs-locally]

```bash
pnpm docs:dev
```

Then open `/en/docs` and `/ko/docs`.


# Agent CLI Contract



RAGit now keeps a clearer split between human-readable CLI output and machine-safe command contracts.
If you are integrating it into an agent workflow, use these rules first.

Default Rules [#default-rules]

* Prefer `--format json` for machine consumers.
* Use `ragit describe <command> --format json` before integrating a command for the first time.
* Prefer `--view minimal` for `query`, `context pack`, and `memory recall`.
* Prefer `--input <path|->` for agent calls that carry structured payloads.
* Run mutating commands with `--dry-run` first.

Contract Shape [#contract-shape]

Most major commands can now return a shared envelope:

```json
{
  "command": "query",
  "ok": true,
  "version": "1.0.1",
  "cwd": "/repo/path",
  "data": {},
  "warnings": []
}
```

That envelope is meant to stay stable even when the human text output changes.

For per-command syntax, payload shapes, and failure notes, use <a href="/en/docs/commands">Commands</a> as the detailed reference.

Read Commands [#read-commands]

* `query` supports positional input or `--input <path|->` JSON payloads.
* `context pack` supports positional input or `--input <path|->` JSON payloads.
* `memory recall` accepts a goal and can shrink output with `--view minimal|default|full`.

For agents, start small:

```bash
ragit query --input query.json --view minimal --format json
ragit context pack --input context-pack.json --view minimal --format json
ragit memory recall "resume auth flow" --view minimal --format json
```

Mutating Commands [#mutating-commands]

These commands now support `--dry-run` so an agent can validate intent before writing:

* `ingest`
* `hooks install`
* `hooks uninstall`
* `memory wrap`
* `memory promote`

Typical pattern:

```bash
ragit memory promote --input promote.json --dry-run --format json
ragit memory promote --input promote.json --format json
```

Memory Caveat [#memory-caveat]

Do not confuse the two memory surfaces:

* `.ragit/memory/**` is the control plane for working state and session history.
* `docs/memory/**` is the searchable corpus that participates in ingest and snapshot retrieval.

That means agents should use `memory wrap` and `memory recall` to manage active work, and `memory promote` only when knowledge is stable enough to become searchable project memory.


# CLI Reference



The detailed CLI reference moved to <a href="/en/docs/commands">Commands</a>.

Use this page only as a stable redirect target for older links and bookmarks.

Where To Go Now [#where-to-go-now]

* Start with <a href="/en/docs/commands">Commands</a> for command families and leaf-command guides.
* Read <a href="/en/docs/agent-cli">Agent CLI Contract</a> for machine-safe integration rules.
* Read <a href="/en/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a> if you need the philosophy behind `memory wrap`, `memory recall`, and `memory promote`.


# Release Notes



v0.1 Highlights [#v01-highlights]

* Full CLI command surface for MVP
* 8 document types including SPEC/PBD
* `SPEC` documents implementation-level functional requirements and contracts
* `PBD` documents implementation phase topology and binding architecture
* Interactive `init` with AGENTS parsing
* Hybrid retrieval and context pack
* sqlite-vss compatibility migration path

Verification [#verification]

* Unit tests for doc type, masking, hybrid score
* Integration tests for incremental ingest and init flows
* Build/test checks pass on CI baseline


# Init Guide



The repository init command is now a discover-first bootstrap flow. It still anchors to the repository root, still prepares `.ragit/**`, `AGENTS.md`, guide assets, and the empty zvec store, but it does that only after it scans the repository and decides what knowledge already exists.

Flow [#flow]

1. Check Git context and optionally run `git init`
2. Scan repository code/docs/build files
3. Select `empty`, `existing`, `docs-heavy`, or `monorepo` mode
4. Run documentation census, coverage scoring, and maturity scoring
5. Build a knowledge-slot map from existing repository sources
6. Plan missing foundational docs and reuse existing sources first
7. Write stage-1 drafts, bootstrap `.ragit/**`, and bootstrap the zvec store
8. Print the final report and next actions

Stage-1 generated docs [#stage-1-generated-docs]

`init` can generate only these draft docs in stage 1:

* `RAGIT.md`
* `docs/workspace-map.md`
* `docs/ragit/ingestion-policy.md`
* `docs/known-gaps.md`
* `docs/adr/README.md`

Every generated doc is marked as inferred draft content so humans can distinguish generated scaffolding from validated repository truth.

What init prepares [#what-init-prepares]

* Git-aware repository normalization
* Existing-doc discovery and coverage evaluation
* Draft foundational docs for missing gaps only
* `.ragit/config.toml`
* `.ragit/guide/templates/*`
* `.ragit/guide/guide-index.json`
* `.ragit/store/meta.json`
* Empty zvec collections for `documents` and `chunks`

What init does not prepare [#what-init-does-not-prepare]

* No searchable corpus
* No chunk/document record creation in zvec
* No snapshot manifests
* No query-ready knowledge state

`init` can inspect repository docs, but searchable knowledge still begins with <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a>.

Representative scenarios [#representative-scenarios]

Empty repository [#empty-repository]

* `pnpm ragit init --yes --git-init`
* Mode selected: `empty`
* Expected result: foundational drafts plus control-plane/bootstrap assets

Existing codebase [#existing-codebase]

* `pnpm ragit init`
* Mode selected: `existing`
* Expected result: existing docs are reused, missing drafts are added, no core docs are overwritten

Docs-heavy repository [#docs-heavy-repository]

* `pnpm ragit init`
* Mode selected: `docs-heavy`
* Expected result: high reuse ratio, low generated-doc count

Monorepo [#monorepo]

* `pnpm ragit init`
* Mode selected: `monorepo`
* Expected result: apps/packages are reflected in the workspace map and knowledge map

Dry-run [#dry-run]

```bash
pnpm ragit init --dry-run --output json
```

Expected result:

* full scan/coverage/maturity report
* planned create/reuse/skip actions
* no file system mutations

Non-interactive examples [#non-interactive-examples]

```bash
pnpm ragit init --yes
pnpm ragit init --yes --git-init
pnpm ragit init --mode auto --strategy balanced --merge-existing --output json
pnpm ragit init --dry-run --output json
```

Generated structure [#generated-structure]

```text
RAGIT.md
docs/
  adr/README.md
  known-gaps.md
  ragit/ingestion-policy.md
  workspace-map.md
.ragit/
  config.toml
  guide/
    guide-index.json
    templates/
  store/
    meta.json
    documents/
    chunks/
```


# Memory Model Guide







RAGit does not treat memory as one giant transcript. It separates memory by purpose so that an agent can resume work without dragging every raw note, tool output, or past conversation back into the prompt.

The Problem [#the-problem]

Agents do not naturally remember your project the way a teammate does. A raw session log can tell you everything that happened, but it is bad at telling you what matters right now.

For a user, the real question is simpler:

* What was I trying to do?
* Which constraints still matter?
* What is still open?
* What knowledge is now stable enough to reuse later?

RAGit splits memory so it can answer those questions directly instead of replaying the entire past.

The Mental Model [#the-mental-model]

<img alt="Three-layer memory diagram showing short-term scratch space, working memory for active continuity, and long-term memory for reusable knowledge." src={__img0} />

This diagram is the fastest mental model for the page. The top layer is temporary, the middle layer is active continuity, and the bottom layer is durable project knowledge. In the current MVP, RAGit puts most of its weight on the middle and bottom layers.

<Cards>
  <Card title="Short-term memory">
    Temporary scratch space for rough notes, partial observations, and in-session thinking. It exists conceptually, but it is not the main searchable layer in the MVP.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Working memory">
    The active state you want to resume: goal, constraints, open loops, and next actions. This layer restores momentum.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Long-term memory">
    Reusable project knowledge such as stable decisions, glossary entries, and durable plans. This layer restores durable knowledge.
  </Card>
</Cards>

Think about memory in three layers:

* **Short-term memory** is scratch space. It holds temporary notes, rough ideas, and in-progress observations inside a session.
* **Working memory** is the active state you want to resume tomorrow. It should tell you the goal, constraints, open loops, and next actions.
* **Long-term memory** is reusable project knowledge. It contains things that have become stable enough to search and reuse across future work.

In the current MVP, RAGit emphasizes the second and third layers. Working memory helps you continue a task. Long-term memory helps you find durable knowledge again later. Short-term memory still exists conceptually, but it remains transient scratch space rather than a first-class searchable layer.

Why Two Places Exist [#why-two-places-exist]

RAGit uses two places because the project needs two different kinds of memory behavior.

* `.ragit/memory/**` is the **control plane**. It stores session wraps and current working state. This area is good for resuming work, but it is not meant to be your general searchable knowledge base.
* `docs/memory/**` is the **searchable corpus**. It stores promoted decisions, glossary entries, and plans as normal documents that can participate in ingest and snapshot-scoped retrieval.

This split protects signal quality. Working memory stays focused on “what is active now,” while long-term memory stays focused on “what should remain findable later.”

Command Flow [#command-flow]

<img alt="Swimlane diagram showing wrap and recall on the control plane and promote plus ingest on the searchable corpus side." src={__img1} />

The left lane is about continuity now. The right lane is about knowledge worth finding later. `memory recall` is the bridge between them: it starts from current working state and then pulls in relevant snapshot-scoped retrieval.

<Callout title="A simple rule of thumb">
  `memory wrap` preserves continuity, `memory recall` restores the smallest useful packet, and `memory promote` crystallizes stable knowledge into searchable project memory.
</Callout>

Exact path sketch [#exact-path-sketch]

```text
CONTROL PLANE (.ragit/memory/**)             SEARCHABLE CORPUS (docs/memory/**)

session work
   |
   +--> memory wrap ------> sessions/ + working/
   |
goal for next session
   |
   +--> memory recall <--- working/ + snapshot retrieval
   |                     --> recall packet
   |
stable knowledge
   |
   +--> memory promote --------------------> decisions/ glossary/ plans
                                              |
                                              +--> ingest --> searchable snapshot hits
```

How Commands Map [#how-commands-map]

* `memory wrap` records the state of the work you want to resume. It writes session history and refreshes the current working state.
* `memory recall` reconstructs the smallest useful packet for continuing work. It starts from what is active now in working memory and then supplements it with relevant snapshot-scoped retrieval results rather than replaying the full past.
* `memory promote` turns stable knowledge into searchable documents. It is how an important conclusion stops being a session detail and becomes project memory. In the current MVP, promoted documents are ingested immediately when `HEAD` is available.

The key idea is that wrap is for continuity, recall is for restoration, and promote is for crystallization.

A Simple Example [#a-simple-example]

Imagine you stop work on an auth migration at the end of the day.

You use `memory wrap` to save the current goal, the rule that snapshot contracts must stay intact, the open loop about token refresh, and the next action for tomorrow. That state lives in `.ragit/memory/**`.

The next morning, you do not want to reread every note. You use `memory recall`. RAGit pulls the current working state and combines it with relevant retrieved knowledge, such as earlier decisions about auth boundaries or plans stored in the repository. You get a recall packet that tells you where to restart.

Later, once a rule becomes stable, such as “refresh token handling must remain outside snapshot mutation,” you use `memory promote`. That rule becomes a durable document in `docs/memory/**`, where future sessions can search it like any other project knowledge.

Common Misunderstandings [#common-misunderstandings]

Why not search .ragit/memory/** directly? [#why-not-search-ragitmemory-directly]

Because working state changes often and exists to help you resume active work. It is operational state, not automatically durable knowledge.

Why is promote separate from wrap? [#why-is-promote-separate-from-wrap]

Because not every session note deserves to become long-term memory. Promotion is the deliberate step that keeps the searchable corpus clean.

When should I promote something? [#when-should-i-promote-something]

Promote it when you expect a future session to benefit from finding it without reopening the original session history. In the current MVP, that usually means a stable decision, a reusable term, or a plan worth searching later.

Is working memory the same as a snapshot? [#is-working-memory-the-same-as-a-snapshot]

No. A snapshot answers, “What knowledge is searchable for this commit?” Working memory answers, “What am I actively trying to continue right now?”

Why keep both working memory and long-term memory? [#why-keep-both-working-memory-and-long-term-memory]

Because they solve different recovery problems. Working memory restores momentum. Long-term memory restores durable knowledge.


# describe



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`describe` returns the current command contract for a specific CLI path. It is the runtime source of truth for command purpose, arguments, options, examples, output fields, and doc metadata.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You are integrating RAGit into an agent or automation.
* You want to confirm whether a command supports `--input`, `--dry-run`, or `--view`.
* You want to detect contract drift before hard-coding a command call.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You already know the command and only want a quick human explanation. Read the corresponding command page instead.
* You want actual project data, not command metadata. Use the target command directly.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit describe <command-path...> [--format text|json|both]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `<command-path...>`: The full command path. Multi-word commands are passed as separate words, for example `memory promote` or `hooks install`.
* `--format text|json|both`: Return a text summary, a JSON envelope, or both.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is positional only.
* JSON output includes `command`, `availableCommands`, and a `spec` object.
* The `spec` object includes `group`, `docSlug`, `relatedCommands`, `stability`, input capabilities, and output fields.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit describe query
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit describe memory promote --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* The command fails if the requested path is not in the describable leaf-command set.
* Use the exact command path. `memory` alone is a family, not a leaf command.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/agent-cli">
    Agent CLI Contract
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">
    `query`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">
    `context pack`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/promote">
    `memory promote`
  </a>


# doctor



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`doctor` runs a multi-check diagnosis across git setup, RAGit structure, config, zvec runtime, schema layout, and manifest consistency.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* A command is failing and you need a structured diagnostic view.
* You suspect a store, schema, or manifest mismatch.
* You want a CI-friendly health check that can exit non-zero on failure.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want a quick current-state summary. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">`status`</a>.
* You already know the issue and need to reindex. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit doctor [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--format text|json|both`: Choose human text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* There is no positional input and no JSON payload input.
* JSON output includes `checks` and `hasFailure`.
* If any check fails, the process exits with a non-zero code after printing the result.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit doctor
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit doctor --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* A failing `doctor` result often signals repository or runtime setup problems outside a single command.
* Use the individual check names in the JSON output when automating follow-up actions.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">
    `status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/init">
    `init`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>


# Commands



The `commands` group is the new home for detailed command documentation.
Each leaf page explains one executable CLI command from the user point of view and the agent integration point of view.

Start Here [#start-here]

* Read <a href="/en/docs/commands/init">`init`</a> when you are setting up RAGit in a repository.
* Read <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">`query`</a> when you want direct retrieval hits from indexed knowledge.
* Read <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">`context pack`</a> when you want a compact prompt packet for the next agent step.
* Read <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a> when you want to resume active work instead of doing a general search.

Command Families [#command-families]

* Top-level commands: <a href="/en/docs/commands/init">`init`</a>, <a href="/en/docs/commands/describe">`describe`</a>, <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a>, <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">`query`</a>, <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">`status`</a>, <a href="/en/docs/commands/doctor">`doctor`</a>
* Config: <a href="/en/docs/commands/config">`config`</a>
* Context: <a href="/en/docs/commands/context">`context`</a>
* Hooks: <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks">`hooks`</a>
* Memory: <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory">`memory`</a>
* Migrate: <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate">`migrate`</a>

How To Read These Pages [#how-to-read-these-pages]

* Every leaf page uses the same structure so you can find syntax, options, payload examples, and cautions quickly.
* Commands that support `--input <path|->` include a JSON payload example.
* Commands that support `--view` explain the difference between `minimal`, `default`, and `full`.
* Commands that support `--dry-run` explain what is validated before any real mutation happens.

Fast Mappings [#fast-mappings]

* `query` answers: "What indexed knowledge matches this question?"
* `context pack` answers: "What is the smallest useful prompt packet for this goal?"
* `memory wrap` answers: "What should I record before stopping?"
* `memory recall` answers: "What should I load before resuming?"
* `memory promote` answers: "What should become durable project knowledge?"

If you still prefer the old entry point, the legacy <a href="/en/docs/cli-reference">CLI Reference</a> page now redirects you into this group.


# ingest



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`ingest` scans supported documents, writes embeddings and records into the canonical store, and produces a snapshot manifest bound to the current commit.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You added or changed project docs and want retrieval to see them.
* You promoted durable memory into `docs/memory/**`.
* You want to preview which files would be indexed before running a real mutation.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only need to search existing indexed data. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">`query`</a>.
* You only need the current working state. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit ingest [--all | --since <sha> | --files <glob> | --input <path|->] [--dry-run] [--format text|json|both]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--all`: Index all supported repository documents.
* `--since <sha>`: Index only changes since a commit.
* `--files <glob>`: Index a targeted glob.
* `--input <path|->`: Provide a structured JSON payload instead of flags.
* `--dry-run`: Validate inputs and compute the plan without writing the store or manifest.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose the result envelope form.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Human mode normally uses `--all`, `--since`, or `--files`.
* Agent mode should prefer `--input <path|->` when multiple selectors are involved.

```json
{
  "all": false,
  "since": "HEAD~1",
  "files": "docs/**/*.md"
}
```

* JSON output reports `mode`, `processed`, `skipped`, `masked`, `commitSha`, `manifestPath`, `plannedFiles`, and `deletedDocumentIds`.
* `--dry-run` returns the same planned surface without mutating the store.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit ingest --all --format both
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit ingest --input ingest.json --dry-run --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Do not mix `--input` with selector flags in the same call.
* Invalid globs and unsafe path patterns are rejected before indexing begins.
* `ingest` only sees searchable corpus docs, not `.ragit/memory/**` control-plane files.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">
    `query`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">
    `context pack`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/promote">
    `memory promote`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">
    `hooks install`
  </a>


# init



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`init` is a discover-first bootstrap command. It normalizes to the repository root, checks Git context, scans the repository, classifies it as `empty`, `existing`, `docs-heavy`, or `monorepo`, evaluates documentation coverage/maturity, plans missing foundational docs, and then bootstraps the control plane and local zvec store.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You are setting up RAGit in a repository for the first time.
* You want existing docs to be reused before new draft docs are generated.
* You want a machine-readable initialization report for agents or CI.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want searchable knowledge. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a> after init.
* You only want current health/status checks. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">`status`</a> or <a href="/en/docs/commands/doctor">`doctor`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit init [--cwd <path>] [--yes|--non-interactive] [--mode auto|empty|existing|monorepo|docs-heavy] [--strategy minimal|balanced|full] [--dry-run] [--merge-existing] [--git-init] [--output text|json|both]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--cwd <path>`: Run initialization against another repository path. If the path is inside a Git worktree, `init` anchors to the repository root.
* `--yes`: Accept defaults and skip prompts.
* `--non-interactive`: Same intent as `--yes`, useful in scripted flows.
* `--mode auto|empty|existing|monorepo|docs-heavy`: Override repository mode detection. Default is `auto`.
* `--strategy minimal|balanced|full`: Control how aggressively stage-1 foundational docs are generated. Default is `balanced`.
* `--dry-run`: Compute the full report without writing files or bootstrapping storage.
* `--merge-existing`: Reuse existing docs as primary sources before creating draft files. This is the default behavior.
* `--git-init`: Create a git repository automatically in non-interactive mode.
* `--output text|json|both`: Choose the human summary, JSON summary, or both.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is flag-based only. There is no `--input <path|->` payload mode.
* JSON output includes:
  * `executionMode`
  * `repositoryMode`
  * `strategy`
  * `scan`
  * `coverage`
  * `maturity`
  * `knowledgeMap`
  * `actions`
  * `bootstrap`
  * `nextActions`
* `actions` reports planned create/reuse/skip outcomes for stage-1 foundational docs.
* `bootstrap` reports the AGENTS/guide/zvec state or planned state during `--dry-run`.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit init --mode auto --strategy balanced --merge-existing
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit init --yes --git-init --output json
```

Preview-only flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit init --dry-run --output json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* In non-interactive mode, `init` fails if the directory is not a git repository and `--git-init` is missing.
* In TTY-less environments, interactive mode is not available.
* `--dry-run` performs repository analysis but does not write files or create the zvec store.
* zvec bootstrap currently supports `darwin/arm64`, `linux/arm64`, and `linux/x64`.
* `init` can inspect repository docs, but it still does **not** make the repository search-ready. Run <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a> after setup.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">
    `status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/doctor">
    `doctor`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">
    `hooks install`
  </a>


# query



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`query` searches indexed repository knowledge and returns matching hits for a question. The result is always snapshot-scoped, so the answer is tied to the chosen commit state.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You want direct retrieval hits for a question.
* You want to inspect what the snapshot knows at a specific commit.
* You want a lightweight agent call before building a larger context packet.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You need a prompt-sized packet with token budgeting. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">`context pack`</a>.
* You want to resume active work from working memory. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit query [question] [--input <path|->] [--top-k <n>] [--at <sha>] [--view minimal|default|full] [--format text|json|both]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `question`: A direct search question.
* `--input <path|->`: Provide a JSON payload with `question`, `topK`, and optional `at`.
* `--top-k <n>`: Control how many hits to return.
* `--at <sha>`: Query a specific snapshot SHA or prefix.
* `--view minimal|default|full`: Choose how much hit detail to project.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose the output form.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Positional input is fine for a quick human call.
* Agents should prefer `--input <path|->` when they need reproducible payloads.

```json
{
  "question": "restore auth context",
  "topK": 5,
  "at": "HEAD"
}
```

* JSON output returns `query`, `snapshotSha`, and `hits`.
* `--view minimal` returns the smallest useful hit shape.
* `--view default` returns the standard readable hit projection.
* `--view full` keeps the richest text projection and is the most expensive for context windows.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit query "restore auth context" --format both
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit query --input query.json --view minimal --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Do not mix `--input` with positional `question`, `--top-k`, or `--at`.
* If `question` is empty, the command fails.
* `query` only sees indexed content that belongs to the snapshot.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">
    `context pack`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">
    `memory recall`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>


# status



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`status` prints the current repository state that matters to RAGit: branch, HEAD, backend, manifest count, embedding config, and zvec availability.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You want a quick operational snapshot of the current repository.
* You want to confirm whether the store is loaded and whether retrieval should be ready.
* You want to inspect config surfaces without opening files manually.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You want a deeper health diagnosis. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/doctor">`doctor`</a>.
* You want to change config values. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/config/set">`config set`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit status [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--format text|json|both`: Choose human text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* There is no positional input and no JSON payload input.
* JSON output includes `branch`, `head`, `backend`, `zvec`, `supported_types`, `manifests`, `embedding`, and `format`.
* Agents should usually call `--format json`.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit status
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit status --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* `status` is a summary, not a repair command.
* A loaded store does not guarantee that your latest docs are indexed. Run <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a> after content changes.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/doctor">
    `doctor`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/config/set">
    `config set`
  </a>


# config



The `config` family controls repository-local RAGit settings.

Choose A Subcommand [#choose-a-subcommand]

* Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/config/set">`config set`</a> to update one config key.

Family Boundary [#family-boundary]

* `config` changes behavior.
* `status` and `doctor` inspect behavior.
* `init` prepares defaults but does not replace targeted config edits.


# config set



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`config set` updates one key in `.ragit/config.toml` using the repository-aware config loader and writer.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You want to change output, ingest, storage, or embedding settings.
* You want a scriptable way to change config without editing TOML by hand.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want to inspect current values. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">`status`</a>.
* You want to initialize missing defaults. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/init">`init`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit config set <key> <value> [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `<key>`: The dotted config key to update.
* `<value>`: The value to store.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is positional only.
* Output is a plain stdout confirmation message. This command does not currently expose a JSON envelope.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit config set output.format markdown
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit config set ingest.supported_types '["adr","spec"]'
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Invalid keys or invalid values fail before the config file is written.
* Because output is text-only, agents should validate follow-up state with <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">`status`</a>.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">
    `status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/doctor">
    `doctor`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/init">
    `init`
  </a>


# context



The `context` family is for assembling prompt-ready packets instead of raw retrieval hits.

Choose A Subcommand [#choose-a-subcommand]

* Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">`context pack`</a> when you know the goal and want a bounded packet.

Family Boundary [#family-boundary]

* `query` gives direct hits.
* `context pack` turns relevant hits into a budgeted packet.
* `memory recall` adds working-memory state on top of retrieval when you are resuming an active task.


# context pack



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`context pack` turns retrieved knowledge into a bounded packet for a specific goal. It is the command to use when the next consumer is an agent prompt rather than a person reading search results.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You have a goal and want a compact context handoff.
* You need token budgeting.
* You want to pass structured input to an agent reliably.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want to inspect raw retrieval hits. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">`query`</a>.
* You want to resume active work with open loops and working state. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit context pack [goal] [--input <path|->] [--budget <tokens>] [--at <sha>] [--view minimal|default|full] [--format text|json|both]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `goal`: The goal that should drive context selection.
* `--input <path|->`: Provide a JSON payload with `goal`, optional `budget`, and optional `at`.
* `--budget <tokens>`: Token budget for the selected packet.
* `--at <sha>`: Use a specific snapshot instead of the current HEAD state.
* `--view minimal|default|full`: Control how much detail is projected into the packet.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

```json
{
  "goal": "implementation plan for auth",
  "budget": 1200,
  "at": "HEAD"
}
```

* JSON output includes `goal`, `snapshotSha`, `budget`, `usedTokens`, `selectedHits`, and `hits`.
* `--view minimal` is the safest default for agent context windows.
* `--view default` preserves a balanced amount of detail.
* `--view full` is useful only when downstream reasoning truly needs richer text.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit context pack "implementation plan for auth" --budget 1200
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit context pack --input context-pack.json --view minimal --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Do not mix `--input` with positional `goal`, `--budget`, or `--at`.
* An empty `goal` is rejected.
* This command remains snapshot-bound; it does not include working-memory state unless you explicitly use <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a>.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">
    `query`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">
    `memory recall`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/describe">
    `describe`
  </a>


# Hooks



The `hooks` family manages the git hooks that RAGit installs under `.git/hooks`.

Choose A Subcommand [#choose-a-subcommand]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">`hooks install`</a>: install managed hooks.
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/uninstall">`hooks uninstall`</a>: remove managed hooks without touching external ones.
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/status">`hooks status`</a>: inspect current hook state.

Family Boundary [#family-boundary]

* Hooks automate ingestion triggers.
* They do not replace explicit <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a> calls when you need a full refresh.


# hooks install



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`hooks install` writes managed `post-commit` and `post-merge` hooks that trigger incremental ingestion.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You want indexing to stay closer to repository changes automatically.
* You just finished `init`.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want to inspect the hook state. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/status">`hooks status`</a>.
* You are removing managed hooks. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/uninstall">`hooks uninstall`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks install [--dry-run] [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--dry-run`: Show what would be installed without writing files.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is flag-based only.
* JSON output includes `dryRun`, `root`, and `hooks`.
* `--dry-run` verifies paths and returns planned hook states without mutation.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks install
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks install --dry-run --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Hooks are written into `.git/hooks`, so the repository must have a valid git root.
* The installed hooks are managed-by-ragit hooks, not arbitrary shell automation.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/status">
    `hooks status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/uninstall">
    `hooks uninstall`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>


# hooks status



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`hooks status` reports whether the expected `post-commit` and `post-merge` hooks are installed, absent, or external.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You want to verify hook state before install or uninstall.
* You want a machine-readable view of hook ownership.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You want to mutate hook state. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">`hooks install`</a> or <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/uninstall">`hooks uninstall`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks status [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is flag-based only.
* JSON output includes `dryRun`, `root`, and `hooks`.
* Each hook item reports `name`, `target`, `action`, and `state`.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks status
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks status --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* External hooks are not an error by themselves; they are a signal to avoid destructive automation.
* `hooks status` does not confirm that the hook target command succeeded in previous runs. It only inspects file state.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">
    `hooks install`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/uninstall">
    `hooks uninstall`
  </a>


# hooks uninstall



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`hooks uninstall` removes only hooks that were installed and marked as managed by RAGit. External hooks are left untouched and reported as external.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You no longer want automatic ingest hooks.
* You need to verify whether managed hooks can be removed safely.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want hook visibility. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/status">`hooks status`</a>.
* You want to install hooks instead. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">`hooks install`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks uninstall [--dry-run] [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--dry-run`: Preview which managed hooks would be removed.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is flag-based only.
* JSON output includes `dryRun`, `root`, and `hooks`.
* `--dry-run` is the safe mode to confirm that external hooks will not be removed.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks uninstall --dry-run
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit hooks uninstall --dry-run --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Hooks not marked with the RAGit header are treated as external and preserved.
* Review the `state` field before a real uninstall if the repository already had custom hooks.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/status">
    `hooks status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/hooks/install">
    `hooks install`
  </a>


# Memory



The `memory` family separates active continuity from durable searchable knowledge.

Choose A Subcommand [#choose-a-subcommand]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/wrap">`memory wrap`</a>: record the current session state.
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a>: rebuild the next-session packet.
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/promote">`memory promote`</a>: crystallize stable knowledge into searchable docs.

Family Boundary [#family-boundary]

* `wrap` writes control-plane state into `.ragit/memory/**`.
* `recall` reads working state and combines it with retrieval.
* `promote` writes durable docs into `docs/memory/**` and can trigger targeted ingest.


# memory promote



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`memory promote` takes promotion candidates and writes durable docs under `docs/memory/**`, such as decisions, glossary entries, or plans. When `HEAD` is available, it then ingests those docs so they become searchable immediately.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* A decision, glossary term, or plan should become durable project memory.
* You want future retrieval to find the result without reopening session history.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* The note is still provisional. Keep it in working memory with <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/wrap">`memory wrap`</a>.
* You only want to resume active work. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">`memory recall`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit memory promote --input <path|-> [--dry-run] [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--input <path|->`: Required JSON payload describing promotion candidates.
* `--dry-run`: Validate candidates, planned target docs, and planned ingest without writing.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

```json
{
  "promotionCandidates": [
    {
      "type": "adr",
      "title": "Keep refresh handling outside manifest mutation",
      "summary": "Separates active auth refresh logic from snapshot writes.",
      "rationale": "Preserves snapshot contract stability.",
      "nextReviewAt": "2026-06-01"
    }
  ]
}
```

* JSON output includes `createdFiles`, `plannedFiles`, `sourceHeadSha`, `ingested`, `dryRun`, and `warnings`.
* `--dry-run` tells you which doc files and ingest targets would be touched.
* If `HEAD` does not exist, promotion still plans file creation but warns that ingest is skipped.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit memory promote --input promotion-batch.json
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit memory promote --input promotion-batch.json --dry-run --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* Promotion is the deliberate filter that keeps `docs/memory/**` clean. Do not use it for raw scratch notes.
* The payload must use valid promotion candidate types and fields.
* Promoted docs affect searchable retrieval after ingest, not before.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/wrap">
    `memory wrap`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/memory-model">
    Memory Model Guide
  </a>


# memory recall



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`memory recall` reconstructs the packet you need to continue active work. It starts from current working memory and then supplements it with relevant snapshot-scoped retrieval hits.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You are resuming an interrupted task.
* You want open loops, constraints, related decisions, and next actions in one packet.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You only want a general repository search. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">`query`</a>.
* You only want a prompt-sized retrieval pack with no working state. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">`context pack`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit memory recall <goal> [--view minimal|default|full] [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `<goal>`: The task you want to resume.
* `--view minimal|default|full`: Control how much retrieved detail is projected into the packet.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is positional only.
* JSON output includes `goal`, `constraints`, `openLoops`, `relatedDecisions`, `retrievedHits`, and `nextActions`.
* `--view minimal` is the best default for agent resumes.
* `--view default` balances readability and detail.
* `--view full` is only for cases where the restored packet must carry richer text from retrieval hits.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit memory recall "resume auth flow"
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit memory recall "resume auth flow" --view minimal --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* `memory recall` depends on working-memory state quality. If wraps are poor, recall quality drops too.
* This command does not replay the entire session history. It intentionally produces a selective packet.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/wrap">
    `memory wrap`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">
    `query`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">
    `context pack`
  </a>


# memory wrap



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`memory wrap` records the current session state into `.ragit/memory/**`. It writes a session wrap, refreshes the current working state, and updates the open-loops registry.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You are stopping work and want tomorrow's restart point preserved.
* You want the current goal, constraints, open loops, and next actions written into working memory.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* You want to search stable project knowledge. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/query">`query`</a> or <a href="/en/docs/commands/context/pack">`context pack`</a>.
* You want to promote stable knowledge into searchable memory. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/promote">`memory promote`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit memory wrap --input <path|-> [--dry-run] [--format text|json|both] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--input <path|->`: Required JSON payload describing the session wrap.
* `--dry-run`: Validate the wrap and show planned writes without changing files.
* `--format text|json|both`: Choose text, JSON, or both.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

```json
{
  "goal": "finish auth migration",
  "summary": "Refined refresh token boundaries and left one open loop.",
  "constraints": ["Do not break snapshot contracts."],
  "decisions": ["Keep refresh handling outside manifest mutation."],
  "openLoops": [
    {
      "id": "auth-refresh",
      "title": "Finish refresh token handling",
      "status": "open",
      "nextAction": "Update the adapter tests"
    }
  ],
  "nextActions": ["Update adapter tests", "Run pnpm test"],
  "promotionCandidates": [],
  "sourceHeadSha": "HEAD",
  "createdAt": "2026-03-08T10:00:00.000Z"
}
```

* JSON output includes `sessionId`, `sessionPath`, `currentPath`, `openLoopsPath`, `sourceHeadSha`, and `dryRun`.
* `--dry-run` validates payload shape and shows target files without writing them.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit memory wrap --input session-wrap.json
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit memory wrap --input - --dry-run --format json
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* The payload must match the wrap schema. Unexpected or malformed fields are rejected.
* `memory wrap` does not ingest anything into searchable retrieval.
* If `HEAD` is missing, wrap can still write state, but it will record the missing source reference accordingly.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/recall">
    `memory recall`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/memory/promote">
    `memory promote`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/memory-model">
    Memory Model Guide
  </a>


# migrate from-json-store



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`migrate from-json-store` reads the legacy JSON-backed store and rewrites its documents, chunks, and snapshot data into the current canonical storage layout.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* `status` or `init` indicates that a legacy JSON store still exists.
* You are upgrading an older repository layout to the current store contract.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* The repository is already using the canonical store.
* Your legacy source is sqlite-vss export data instead. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate/from-sqlitevss">`migrate from-sqlitevss`</a>.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit migrate from-json-store [--dry-run] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--dry-run`: Count what would be migrated without writing the canonical store.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is flag-based only.
* Output is a raw JSON object with `mode`, `docs`, `chunks`, and optional `snapshotSha`.
* `--dry-run` is the safe first call for automation.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit migrate from-json-store --dry-run
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit migrate from-json-store --dry-run
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* The command fails if the legacy JSON store file is missing.
* Treat migration as a write operation even though it prints raw JSON instead of the shared CLI envelope.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate/from-sqlitevss">
    `migrate from-sqlitevss`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">
    `status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>


# migrate from-sqlitevss



What It Does [#what-it-does]

`migrate from-sqlitevss` reads exported sqlite-vss records and converts them into the canonical document, chunk, and snapshot layout used by current RAGit.

When To Use / When Not To Use [#when-to-use--when-not-to-use]

When to use it [#when-to-use-it]

* You have one of the supported sqlite-vss export files in the repository.
* You are upgrading from an older vector-storage experiment to the current layout.

When not to use it [#when-not-to-use-it]

* Your legacy source is the JSON store instead. Use <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate/from-json-store">`migrate from-json-store`</a>.
* The repository already uses the current canonical store.

Syntax [#syntax]

```bash
pnpm ragit migrate from-sqlitevss [--dry-run] [--cwd <path>]
```

Arguments And Options [#arguments-and-options]

* `--dry-run`: Count convertible docs and chunks without writing.
* `--cwd <path>`: Run against another repository.

Input And Output Contract [#input-and-output-contract]

* Input is flag-based only.
* Output is a raw JSON object with `mode`, `docs`, `chunks`, and optional `snapshotSha`.
* `--dry-run` is the right default for agent automation.

Examples [#examples]

Human terminal flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit migrate from-sqlitevss --dry-run
```

Agent-oriented flow:

```bash
pnpm ragit migrate from-sqlitevss --dry-run
```

Failures And Cautions [#failures-and-cautions]

* The command fails if no supported sqlite-vss export file can be found.
* Like the JSON-store migration, this is a mutating command even though its output is raw JSON.

Related Commands [#related-commands]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate/from-json-store">
    `migrate from-json-store`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">
    `status`
  </a>
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">
    `ingest`
  </a>


# Migrate



The `migrate` family exists for one-time or occasional legacy data conversion.

Choose A Subcommand [#choose-a-subcommand]

* <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate/from-json-store">`migrate from-json-store`</a>: convert the old JSON store.
* <a href="/en/docs/commands/migrate/from-sqlitevss">`migrate from-sqlitevss`</a>: convert exported sqlite-vss data.

Family Boundary [#family-boundary]

* Migrations are write operations.
* After a successful migration, you still verify with <a href="/en/docs/commands/status">`status`</a> or continue with <a href="/en/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a>.


# 아키텍처



런타임 [#런타임]

* TypeScript / Node.js CLI
* `.ragit/store/` 기준 zvec canonical store
* `.ragit/memory/` 기준 메모리 제어면
* `docs/memory/` 기준 검색 가능한 장기기억 코퍼스
* v1 로컬 placeholder 임베딩 provider
* `.ragit/manifest/` Git 추적 스냅샷

검색 [#검색]

* 청크 임베딩 + 키워드 점수 결합
* 가중 하이브리드 랭킹(`alpha`)
* snapshot SHA 기반 시점 조회

결속 모델 [#결속-모델]

* 각 스냅샷은 commit SHA와 결속
* `--since <sha>` 증분 인덱싱 지원
* `post-commit`, `post-merge` 훅 자동 동기화
* `memory wrap`은 snapshot 계약을 바꾸지 않고 working state만 갱신
* `memory promote`는 `docs/memory/**` 문서를 만들고 현재 `HEAD` 기준으로 선택적 ingest 가능

구현 관점이 아니라 사용자 관점의 설명이 필요하다면 <a href="/ko/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a>를 참고하세요.


# 개요



RAGit은 git 기반 AI Agent 워크플로를 위한 로컬 우선 RAG CLI입니다.
프로젝트 문서/컨텍스트를 수집하고 commit SHA와 결속된 스냅샷 검색을 제공합니다.

프로젝트 목적 [#프로젝트-목적]

RAGit은 AI Agent 프로젝트의 문서와 컨텍스트를 저장소 내부에서 커밋 결속형 재사용 지식으로 전환하는 로컬 우선 RAG CLI입니다.

코어 벨류 [#코어-벨류]

* 맥락 보존
* 커밋 결속 재현성
* 에이전트 친화 구조화
* 저마찰 자동화

라이선스 [#라이선스]

RAGit은 Apache-2.0으로 라이선스됩니다.
저장소 전체 라이선스 조건의 단일 기준 문서는 루트 [LICENSE](https://github.com/rhiokim/ragit/blob/main/LICENSE) 파일입니다.

제공 기능 [#제공-기능]

* 커밋 결속 스냅샷
* 하이브리드 검색(벡터 + 키워드)
* 에이전트 컨텍스트 패킹
* 활성 작업과 장기지식을 분리하는 메모리 모델
* 비밀정보 마스킹 수집

지원 문서 타입 [#지원-문서-타입]

문서 타입은 저장 포맷이 아니라 협업 기억 안에서의 역할 구분입니다. RAGit은 이 구분을 사용해 다음 에이전트가 지금 읽는 문서가 제품 의도인지, 시스템 요구사항인지, 구현 계약인지, 결정 기록인지, 위상 설명인지를 더 빠르게 판단하게 합니다.
아래 축약어는 canonical document name의 약칭이므로, 처음 읽을 때는 원래 용어와 약어를 함께 보면서 역할과 이름을 동시에 익히는 편이 좋습니다.

| 타입                                    | 언제 쓰는가                                     | 산출물 초점                | 자주 헷갈리는 이웃 타입                                         |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| `Architecture Decision (ADR)`         | 어떤 결정을 왜 내렸는지 남겨야 할 때                      | 결정, 근거, 결과            | 실행 순서를 적는 `Plan`과 다릅니다                                |
| `Product Requirement (PRD)`           | 제품 문제, 사용자, 목표, 성공 기준을 정의할 때               | 제품 의도와 수용 결과          | 시스템 계약인 `Software Requirements (SRS)`와 다릅니다           |
| `Software Requirements (SRS)`         | 시스템이 무엇을 해야 하는지 정의할 때                      | 기능/비기능 요구사항           | 구현 상세 계약인 `Implementation Specification (SPEC)`와 다릅니다 |
| `Implementation Specification (SPEC)` | 모듈 동작, 인터페이스, 상태, 검증 기준을 고정할 때             | 구현 계약                 | 위상 설명 문서인 `Phase and Binding Documents (PBD)`와 다릅니다   |
| `Plan`                                | 마일스톤, 작업 분해, 실행 순서를 정리할 때                  | 실행 계획과 작업 구조          | 안정화된 결정 기록인 `ADR`와 다릅니다                               |
| `Domain-Driven Design (DDD)`          | bounded context, aggregate, 도메인 경계를 모델링할 때 | 도메인 구조와 개념 경계         | 용어 사전인 `Glossary`와 다릅니다                               |
| `Glossary`                            | 반복되는 용어의 의미를 하나로 고정해야 할 때                  | 공통 어휘                 | 구조/경계를 설명하는 `DDD`와 다릅니다                               |
| `Phase and Binding Documents (PBD)`   | 위상 구조, 결속, 상호작용 경로, 드리프트 지점을 설명할 때         | topology와 binding map | 기능 계약 문서인 `Implementation Specification (SPEC)`와 다릅니다 |

빠른 구분 기준 [#빠른-구분-기준]

* `Product Requirement (PRD)` vs `Software Requirements (SRS)`: `PRD`는 왜 이 제품/기능이 필요한지와 어떤 결과가 중요한지를 다룹니다. `SRS`는 시스템이 무엇을 제공해야 하는지를 다룹니다.
* `Software Requirements (SRS)` vs `Implementation Specification (SPEC)`: `SRS`는 시스템 수준 계약이고, `SPEC`는 구현체 수준 동작과 인터페이스 계약입니다.
* `Implementation Specification (SPEC)` vs `Phase and Binding Documents (PBD)`: `SPEC`는 한 구현 단위가 무엇을 해야 하는지 설명하고, `PBD`는 여러 위상과 결속이 어떻게 연결되는지 설명합니다.
* `Architecture Decision (ADR)` vs `Plan`: `ADR`는 결정 자체를 남기고, `Plan`은 실행 순서를 남깁니다.
* `Domain-Driven Design (DDD)` vs `Glossary`: `DDD`는 도메인 구조와 경계를 모델링하고, `Glossary`는 용어 의미를 고정합니다.

왜 RAGit은 SPEC와 PBD를 분리하는가 [#왜-ragit은-spec와-pbd를-분리하는가]

* `SPEC`는 "이 구현체는 무엇을 해야 하는가?" 같은 기능 계약 질문에 답하기 좋습니다.
* `PBD`는 "이 단계들은 어떻게 연결되는가?" 또는 "어디서 드리프트가 나는가?" 같은 위상 질문에 답하기 좋습니다.
* 두 타입을 분리하면 에이전트가 기능 계약 검색과 topology 검색을 더 정확하게 나눠서 수행할 수 있습니다.

무엇부터 쓰면 좋은가 [#무엇부터-쓰면-좋은가]

* 새 기능이나 워크플로를 정의할 때는 `PRD`에서 시작하고, 필요하면 `SRS`, `SPEC`로 내려가십시오.
* 이미 기능 방향은 정해졌고 한 구현 단위의 동작을 명확히 해야 한다면 `SPEC`부터 쓰십시오.
* 어려운 지점이 위상 연결, 단계 간 결속, 실패 지점이라면 `PBD`를 추가하십시오.
* 대안을 비교해 하나를 확정해야 한다면 `ADR`를 쓰십시오.
* 팀 안에서 같은 용어를 서로 다르게 쓰고 있다면 `Glossary`를, 경계 자체가 흐리다면 `DDD`를 우선 쓰십시오.

다음 단계 [#다음-단계]

<a href="/ko/docs/quickstart">Quickstart</a> 문서에서 실행 절차를 확인하세요.
저장소 안에서 authoring system을 어떻게 준비하는지 보려면 <a href="/ko/docs/init-guide">Init Guide</a>를 읽어보세요.
명령어별 예시와 옵션 설명이 필요하시면 <a href="/ko/docs/commands">Commands</a>부터 보시면 됩니다.
왜 메모리를 control plane과 searchable docs로 나누는지 알고 싶다면 <a href="/ko/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a>를 읽어보세요.
에이전트 워크플로에 RAGit을 넣고 싶다면 <a href="/ko/docs/agent-cli">Agent CLI Contract</a>에서 `--format json`, `--view minimal`, `--input`, `--dry-run` 규약을 먼저 확인하세요.


# 빠른 시작



요구 사항 [#요구-사항]

* Node.js 20+
* pnpm 10+
* Git 저장소

설치 [#설치]

저장소 로컬 개발용:

```bash
pnpm install
```

저장소 체크아웃 안에서는 선택한 package manager에 맞는 프로젝트 스크립트 엔트리로 CLI 명령을 실행합니다.
로컬 CLI 엔트리가 올바르게 연결됐는지 확인하려면 다음 명령을 실행합니다.

```bash
pnpm ragit --help
```

로컬 build 단계는 선택 사항이며, `dist/` 번들을 직접 생성하거나 검증할 때만 필요합니다.

```bash
pnpm build
```

배포된 CLI 설치:

```bash
npm install -g ragit
pnpm add -g ragit
bun add -g ragit
npx ragit --help
```

RAGit 초기화 [#ragit-초기화]

```bash
pnpm ragit init
pnpm ragit hooks install
pnpm ragit ingest --all
```

여기까지 하면 커밋 결속 검색은 바로 사용할 수 있습니다. `memory wrap`, `memory recall`, `memory promote`가 왜 분리되어 있는지 이해하고 싶다면, 프로젝트에 memory 워크플로를 넣기 전에 <a href="/ko/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a>를 먼저 읽으세요.
세부 구문과 명령별 예시가 필요하시면 <a href="/ko/docs/commands">Commands</a>로 이어서 보시면 됩니다.

문서 사이트 로컬 실행 [#문서-사이트-로컬-실행]

```bash
pnpm docs:dev
```

브라우저에서 `/en/docs` 및 `/ko/docs`를 확인합니다.


# Init 가이드



저장소 init 명령은 이제 discover-first bootstrap 흐름입니다. 저장소 루트 정규화, `.ragit/**` 준비, `AGENTS.md`, guide 자산, 빈 zvec store bootstrap은 유지하지만, 그 전에 먼저 저장소를 스캔해서 무엇을 이미 알고 있는지 판정합니다.

흐름 [#흐름]

1. Git 문맥 확인 및 필요 시 `git init`
2. 저장소 코드/문서/빌드 파일 스캔
3. `empty`, `existing`, `docs-heavy`, `monorepo` 모드 판정
4. 문서 census, coverage 평가, maturity 평가 수행
5. 기존 저장소 소스로 knowledge-slot map 구성
6. 누락된 기초 문서를 계획하고 기존 문서를 먼저 재사용
7. 1단계 draft 문서 작성, `.ragit/**` bootstrap, zvec store bootstrap
8. 최종 리포트와 next actions 출력

1단계 생성 문서 [#1단계-생성-문서]

`init`은 1단계에서 아래 draft 문서만 생성할 수 있습니다.

* `RAGIT.md`
* `docs/workspace-map.md`
* `docs/ragit/ingestion-policy.md`
* `docs/known-gaps.md`
* `docs/adr/README.md`

생성 문서는 모두 inferred draft로 표시되므로, 사람이 검증한 저장소 문서와 쉽게 구분할 수 있습니다.

init이 준비하는 것 [#init이-준비하는-것]

* Git 인지 저장소 루트 정규화
* 기존 문서 탐지와 coverage 평가
* 부족한 영역만 메우는 draft foundational docs
* `.ragit/config.toml`
* `.ragit/guide/templates/*`
* `.ragit/guide/guide-index.json`
* `.ragit/store/meta.json`
* `documents`, `chunks` 빈 zvec 컬렉션

init이 준비하지 않는 것 [#init이-준비하지-않는-것]

* searchable corpus 생성 없음
* zvec document/chunk 레코드 생성 없음
* snapshot manifest 생성 없음
* query-ready knowledge state 생성 없음

즉, `init`은 저장소 문서를 읽고 분류할 수는 있지만, searchable knowledge는 여전히 <a href="/ko/docs/commands/ingest">`ingest`</a>부터 시작합니다.

대표 시나리오 [#대표-시나리오]

비어 있는 저장소 [#비어-있는-저장소]

* `pnpm ragit init --yes --git-init`
* 선택 모드: `empty`
* 기대 결과: foundational draft + control-plane/bootstrap 자산 생성

기존 코드베이스 [#기존-코드베이스]

* `pnpm ragit init`
* 선택 모드: `existing`
* 기대 결과: 기존 문서 재사용, 누락된 draft만 추가, 핵심 문서 덮어쓰기 없음

문서가 풍부한 저장소 [#문서가-풍부한-저장소]

* `pnpm ragit init`
* 선택 모드: `docs-heavy`
* 기대 결과: 재사용 비율 높음, 생성 문서 수 낮음

모노레포 [#모노레포]

* `pnpm ragit init`
* 선택 모드: `monorepo`
* 기대 결과: apps/packages 구조가 workspace map과 knowledge map에 반영됨

Dry-run [#dry-run]

```bash
pnpm ragit init --dry-run --output json
```

기대 결과:

* 전체 scan/coverage/maturity report 반환
* create/reuse/skip 계획 반환
* 파일 시스템 변경 없음

비대화형 예시 [#비대화형-예시]

```bash
pnpm ragit init --yes
pnpm ragit init --yes --git-init
pnpm ragit init --mode auto --strategy balanced --merge-existing --output json
pnpm ragit init --dry-run --output json
```

생성 구조 [#생성-구조]

```text
RAGIT.md
docs/
  adr/README.md
  known-gaps.md
  ragit/ingestion-policy.md
  workspace-map.md
.ragit/
  config.toml
  guide/
    guide-index.json
    templates/
  store/
    meta.json
    documents/
    chunks/
```


# Memory Model Guide







RAGit은 메모리를 거대한 대화 원문 한 덩어리로 다루지 않습니다. 메모리를 용도별로 분리해서, 에이전트가 모든 원시 로그와 도구 출력, 과거 대화를 다시 프롬프트에 밀어 넣지 않고도 작업을 이어갈 수 있게 만듭니다.

The Problem [#the-problem]

에이전트는 팀원이 프로젝트를 기억하듯이 자연스럽게 기억하지 못합니다. 세션 원문 로그는 “무슨 일이 있었는지”는 알려줄 수 있지만, “지금 무엇이 중요한지”를 바로 알려주기에는 적합하지 않습니다.

사용자 입장에서 진짜 필요한 질문은 더 단순합니다.

* 내가 하려던 일은 무엇이었는가?
* 아직 지켜야 할 제약은 무엇인가?
* 끝나지 않은 일은 무엇인가?
* 나중에도 다시 써야 할 지식은 무엇인가?

RAGit은 이 질문들에 직접 답하기 위해 메모리를 분리합니다. 과거 전체를 재생하는 대신, 다시 이어가기 위한 구조를 만드는 방식입니다.

The Mental Model [#the-mental-model]

<img alt="임시 작업대, 활성 작업 상태, 장기 프로젝트 지식의 세 층으로 메모리를 나누어 보여주는 다이어그램" src={__img0} />

이 그림이 이 문서의 가장 빠른 정신 모델입니다. 위층은 임시 작업대, 가운데층은 지금 이어야 할 작업 상태, 아래층은 나중에도 다시 찾아야 할 프로젝트 지식입니다. 현재 MVP에서 RAGit은 특히 가운데와 아래층에 더 많은 무게를 둡니다.

<Cards>
  <Card title="Short-term memory">
    세션 중간의 임시 메모, 거친 아이디어, 아직 정리되지 않은 관찰이 머무는 작업대입니다. 개념적으로 존재하지만, 현재 MVP의 핵심 검색 계층은 아닙니다.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Working memory">
    목표, 제약, 열린 루프, 다음 행동처럼 내일 다시 이어야 할 활성 상태입니다. 이 계층은 작업의 관성을 복원합니다.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Long-term memory">
    안정화된 결정, 용어, 계획처럼 나중에도 다시 찾아야 할 프로젝트 지식입니다. 이 계층은 오래 가는 지식을 복원합니다.
  </Card>
</Cards>

메모리는 세 층으로 생각하면 이해하기 쉽습니다.

* **Short-term memory**는 작업대입니다. 세션 중간의 임시 메모, 거친 아이디어, 아직 정리되지 않은 관찰이 머무는 곳입니다.
* **Working memory**는 내일 다시 이어야 할 활성 상태입니다. 목표, 제약, 열린 루프, 다음 행동이 여기에 있어야 합니다.
* **Long-term memory**는 나중에도 다시 찾아야 할 프로젝트 지식입니다. 안정화된 결정, 용어, 계획이 여기에 들어갑니다.

현재 MVP에서 RAGit은 두 번째와 세 번째 층을 더 강하게 다룹니다. Working memory는 작업을 다시 이어가기 위한 것이고, long-term memory는 검증된 지식을 다시 찾기 위한 것입니다. Short-term memory는 개념적으로 존재하지만, 아직은 1급 검색 계층이 아니라 일시적인 작업대에 더 가깝습니다.

Why Two Places Exist [#why-two-places-exist]

RAGit이 메모리를 두 곳에 두는 이유는, 프로젝트가 서로 다른 두 종류의 메모리 동작을 필요로 하기 때문입니다.

* `.ragit/memory/**`는 **control plane**입니다. 세션 wrap과 현재 working state를 저장합니다. 이 영역은 작업 재개에는 적합하지만, 일반 검색용 지식 베이스로 쓰기 위한 곳은 아닙니다.
* `docs/memory/**`는 **searchable corpus**입니다. 승격된 결정, 용어, 계획을 일반 문서처럼 저장하고 ingest와 snapshot 검색에 참여시킵니다.

이 분리는 신호 대 잡음비를 보호합니다. Working memory는 “지금 무엇이 살아 있는가”에 집중하고, long-term memory는 “나중에도 찾아야 할 것”에 집중합니다.

Command Flow [#command-flow]

<img alt="control plane의 wrap과 recall, searchable corpus의 promote와 ingest 관계를 보여주는 swimlane 다이어그램" src={__img1} />

왼쪽 레인은 지금 작업을 다시 이어가기 위한 연속성이고, 오른쪽 레인은 나중에도 찾아야 할 지식입니다. `memory recall`은 이 둘 사이의 다리 역할을 하며, 현재 working state를 먼저 잡고 그 위에 관련 snapshot retrieval 결과를 얹습니다.

<Callout title="간단한 판단 기준">
  `memory wrap`은 연속성을 보존하고, `memory recall`은 최소한의 재진입 패킷을 복원하며, `memory promote`는 안정화된 지식을 searchable project memory로 결정화합니다.
</Callout>

Exact path sketch [#exact-path-sketch]

```text
CONTROL PLANE (.ragit/memory/**)             SEARCHABLE CORPUS (docs/memory/**)

session work
   |
   +--> memory wrap ------> sessions/ + working/
   |
next-session goal
   |
   +--> memory recall <--- working/ + snapshot retrieval
   |                     --> recall packet
   |
stable knowledge
   |
   +--> memory promote --------------------> decisions/ glossary/ plans
                                              |
                                              +--> ingest --> searchable snapshot hits
```

How Commands Map [#how-commands-map]

* `memory wrap`은 나중에 다시 이어야 할 작업 상태를 기록합니다. 세션 히스토리를 저장하고 현재 working state를 갱신합니다.
* `memory recall`은 작업 재개에 필요한 최소 패킷을 복원합니다. 과거 전체를 재생하는 대신, 지금 활성화된 working state를 먼저 잡고 여기에 관련 있는 snapshot-scoped retrieval 결과를 보탭니다.
* `memory promote`는 안정화된 지식을 searchable document로 바꿉니다. 세션의 세부사항이 프로젝트 기억으로 결정화되는 단계입니다. 현재 MVP에서는 `HEAD`가 있으면 promote된 문서를 바로 ingest합니다.

핵심은 이렇습니다. wrap은 연속성을 위한 것이고, recall은 복원을 위한 것이며, promote는 결정화를 위한 것입니다.

A Simple Example [#a-simple-example]

하루가 끝날 때 auth migration 작업을 멈춘다고 가정해 보겠습니다.

`memory wrap`을 사용하면 현재 목표, snapshot 계약은 유지해야 한다는 제약, token refresh 관련 열린 루프, 내일의 다음 행동을 저장할 수 있습니다. 이 상태는 `.ragit/memory/**`에 남습니다.

다음 날 아침에는 모든 메모를 다시 읽고 싶지 않습니다. `memory recall`을 사용하면 현재 working state와 함께, 저장소 안의 관련 결정이나 계획 문서 같은 검색 결과를 묶어서 가져옵니다. 이렇게 만들어진 recall packet이 어디서부터 다시 시작해야 하는지를 알려줍니다.

이후 어떤 규칙이 충분히 안정화되면, 예를 들어 “refresh token 처리는 snapshot mutation 밖에 남겨야 한다” 같은 결론이 생기면 `memory promote`를 사용합니다. 그 규칙은 `docs/memory/**` 아래의 장기기억 문서가 되고, 이후 세션에서 일반 프로젝트 지식처럼 다시 검색할 수 있습니다.

Common Misunderstandings [#common-misunderstandings]

왜 .ragit/memory/**를 바로 검색하지 않나요? [#왜-ragitmemory를-바로-검색하지-않나요]

Working state는 자주 바뀌고, 살아 있는 작업을 다시 이어주기 위한 운영 상태이기 때문입니다. 자동으로 장기지식이 되어야 하는 영역이 아닙니다.

왜 promote를 wrap과 분리하나요? [#왜-promote를-wrap과-분리하나요]

모든 세션 메모가 장기기억이 될 필요는 없기 때문입니다. Promote는 searchable corpus를 깨끗하게 유지하기 위한 의도적인 승격 단계입니다.

언제 promote해야 하나요? [#언제-promote해야-하나요]

미래 세션이 원래 세션 히스토리를 다시 열지 않아도 그 내용을 찾아야 할 때 승격하면 됩니다. 현재 MVP 기준으로는 안정된 결정, 재사용할 용어, 나중에도 검색할 가치가 있는 계획이 대표적입니다.

working memory와 snapshot은 같은 것인가요? [#working-memory와-snapshot은-같은-것인가요]

아닙니다. Snapshot은 “이 커밋에서 무엇이 검색 가능한가?”에 답하고, working memory는 “지금 내가 무엇을 이어가고 있는가?”에 답합니다.

왜 working memory와 long-term memory를 둘 다 유지하나요? [#왜-working-memory와-long-term-memory를-둘-다-유지하나요]

두 계층이 복원하는 문제가 다르기 때문입니다. Working memory는 작업의 관성을 복원하고, long-term memory는 오래 가는 지식을 복원합니다.


# Agent CLI Contract



RAGit은 이제 사람용 출력과 기계용 계약을 더 분명하게 분리합니다.
에이전트 워크플로에 붙인다면 아래 규칙부터 기준으로 삼으시면 됩니다.

기본 규칙 [#기본-규칙]

* 기계 호출은 기본적으로 `--format json`을 우선합니다.
* 처음 붙이는 명령은 `ragit describe <command> --format json`으로 계약부터 읽습니다.
* `query`, `context pack`, `memory recall`은 기본적으로 `--view minimal`을 우선합니다.
* 구조화된 payload가 있으면 `--input <path|->`를 우선합니다.
* mutating 명령은 먼저 `--dry-run`으로 검증합니다.

계약 형태 [#계약-형태]

주요 명령은 공통 envelope를 반환할 수 있습니다.

```json
{
  "command": "query",
  "ok": true,
  "version": "1.0.1",
  "cwd": "/repo/path",
  "data": {},
  "warnings": []
}
```

사람용 텍스트 출력이 바뀌더라도, 에이전트는 이 envelope를 안정적인 계약으로 읽으면 됩니다.

명령별 구문, payload 예시, 실패 주의사항은 <a href="/ko/docs/commands">Commands</a>에서 자세히 보실 수 있습니다.

조회 계열 명령 [#조회-계열-명령]

* `query`는 positional 입력과 `--input <path|->` JSON payload를 모두 지원합니다.
* `context pack`도 positional 입력과 `--input <path|->` JSON payload를 모두 지원합니다.
* `memory recall`은 goal을 받아 `--view minimal|default|full`로 출력량을 조절할 수 있습니다.

에이전트에서는 먼저 작은 패킷부터 사용하십시오.

```bash
ragit query --input query.json --view minimal --format json
ragit context pack --input context-pack.json --view minimal --format json
ragit memory recall "resume auth flow" --view minimal --format json
```

변경 계열 명령 [#변경-계열-명령]

아래 명령은 `--dry-run`을 지원하므로, 에이전트가 쓰기 전에 먼저 계획을 검증할 수 있습니다.

* `ingest`
* `hooks install`
* `hooks uninstall`
* `memory wrap`
* `memory promote`

권장 패턴은 다음과 같습니다.

```bash
ragit memory promote --input promote.json --dry-run --format json
ragit memory promote --input promote.json --format json
```

Memory 주의점 [#memory-주의점]

두 메모리 표면을 혼동하지 마십시오.

* `.ragit/memory/**`는 working state와 session history를 위한 control plane입니다.
* `docs/memory/**`는 ingest와 snapshot retrieval에 참여하는 searchable corpus입니다.

즉, 에이전트는 현재 작업을 다룰 때 `memory wrap`과 `memory recall`을 사용하고, 충분히 안정화된 지식만 `memory promote`로 searchable project memory로 승격해야 합니다.


# CLI 레퍼런스



상세 CLI 레퍼런스는 이제 <a href="/ko/docs/commands">Commands</a>로 이동했습니다.

이 페이지는 기존 링크와 북마크를 깨지 않기 위한 호환 경로만 제공합니다.

지금 어디로 가면 되는가 [#지금-어디로-가면-되는가]

* 명령군과 leaf 명령 상세는 <a href="/ko/docs/commands">Commands</a>를 보시면 됩니다.
* 에이전트 안전 호출 규칙은 <a href="/ko/docs/agent-cli">Agent CLI Contract</a>를 보시면 됩니다.
* `memory wrap`, `memory recall`, `memory promote`의 설계 철학은 <a href="/ko/docs/memory-model">Memory Model Guide</a>에서 확인하실 수 있습니다.


# 릴리즈 노트



v0.1 하이라이트 [#v01-하이라이트]

* MVP CLI 명령 세트 구현
* SPEC/PBD 포함 8개 문서 타입 지원
* `SPEC`로 구현체 수준 상세 기능 요구사항과 계약을 구조화
* `PBD`로 구현체의 위상 구조와 결속 아키텍처를 문서화
* AGENTS 파싱 기반 인터랙티브 `init`
* 하이브리드 검색 및 컨텍스트 패킹
* sqlite-vss 호환 마이그레이션 경로 제공

검증 [#검증]

* 문서 타입/마스킹/검색 점수 단위 테스트
* 증분 인제스트 및 init 통합 테스트
* 빌드/테스트 기준 통과


# describe



무엇을 하는 명령인가 [#무엇을-하는-명령인가]

`describe`는 특정 CLI 경로의 현재 명령 계약을 반환합니다. 명령 목적, 인자, 옵션, 예시, 출력 필드, 문서 메타데이터까지 런타임 기준으로 확인할 수 있습니다.

언제 쓰는가 / 언제 쓰지 않는가 [#언제-쓰는가--언제-쓰지-않는가]

언제 쓰는가 [#언제-쓰는가]

* RAGit을 에이전트나 자동화에 연동할 때
* 어떤 명령이 `--input`, `--dry-run`, `--view`를 지원하는지 확인할 때
* 명령 호출을 코드에 고정하기 전에 계약 변동 여부를 확인할 때

언제 쓰지 않는가 [#언제-쓰지-않는가]

* 이미 명령을 알고 있고 빠른 설명만 필요할 때. 해당 명령 상세 문서를 읽으십시오.
* 실제 프로젝트 데이터를 원할 때. 대상 명령을 직접 실행하십시오.

기본 구문 [#기본-구문]

